figures.caption: Fig. 3: Simulated mobile manipulation results in shared-data regime. Left: Sample efficiency across simulated tasks — symmetry-informed policies (SymAug, EquivReg, EquivNet) yield on average a 2× gain over the symmetry-agnostic baseline (BL). Right: Performance improvements of symmetry-informed policies over baseline increase with task difficulty.

Fig. 2: Reflection symmetry of the Push-T environment [1], illustrating a rollout starting from state s0 (left) and the corresponding reflected rollout starting from the reflected state gr ▷S s0 (right). The expert policy distribution (orange) is reflection-invariant, motivating the use of this symmetry as an inductive bias for policy learning.
